text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 17 December 2023

Dr Jonathan Hale
South Quay Health Centre

12 Mill Quay, Deptford, London SE8 3FU
Dear Dr Jonathan Hale
Thomas Reid, DOB: 14-03-1996, Hospital No: R839274 NHS No. 7410926653
Flat 3, 27 Brookmill Road, London SE8 4JN 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, drug-resistant, with generalised tonic–clonic seizures, absences, focal non-motor events with disorientation, and drop attacks. Mood and sleep disturbance in the context of examination and professional assessment stress; reported deterioration in overall day-to-day functioning and resilience.

Imaging and investigations: Previous MRI reported as non-lesional. Prior routine EEGs with intermittent focal slowing; no recent video-EEG telemetry. No new structural risk factors reported.

Previous and current anti-seizure medications: History includes levetiracetam, carbamazepine, valproate, phenytoin, phenobarbital, gabapentin, clobazam, lacosamide, and zonisamide. Currently taking lamotrigine and perampanel; adherence reported, though variable timing noted during recent exam period. No known medication allergies.

Interim history: I reviewed Thomas Reid in clinic today; he attended alone. He reports a challenging exam/professional assessment season with heightened stress, markedly disturbed sleep, and irregular meals. He describes increased post-ictal fatigue, prolonged recovery times after convulsive seizures, and more pronounced cognitive fog affecting study and work performance. He also reports multiple near-falls at home, with one witnessed minor head knock without loss of consciousness, and escalating anxiety regarding unpredictability of episodes. Occupational disruption has increased, with missed deadlines and reduced capacity to commute independently.

Seizure summary: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. In the context of the above, although headline frequency is unchanged, he perceives deterioration due to greater seizure clustering around stress points, longer post-ictal recovery, and increased functional impact on safety, study, and activities of daily living.

Co-morbidities and relevant history: Mild asthma (inhaler PRN). No recent infections. Sleeps 4–5 hours on average during assessments. Caffeine intake increased. No alcohol misuse. Lives with a flatmate; does not currently drive.

Examination (today): Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. No focal neurological deficits. Cardiorespiratory exam normal. No tongue laceration or shoulder tenderness today. Weight stable.

Current medications (patient-reported):
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.
- Perampanel 6 mg at night.
- Folic acid 5 mg daily.
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN.

Adverse effects: Reports intermittent dizziness and word-finding difficulty, more noticeable during the high-stress period. No new rash, no visual blurring, no mood lability beyond situational anxiety.

Safety and psychosocial: Increased falls risk reported. Using a shower chair and avoids baths. Flatmate aware of seizure first aid. He has contacted the university disability support team; exam accommodations pending. He expresses concern about worsening day-to-day coping despite stable numerical seizure counts.

Plan:
- We discussed the interaction between stress, sleep deprivation, and seizure control. He will prioritise regular sleep and meals, with alarms to stabilise lamotrigine timing.
- I have arranged an epilepsy specialist nurse telephone review in 2–3 weeks to reassess support needs during the ongoing assessment period and to reinforce safety planning.
- Request community occupational therapy home safety assessment given reported near-falls and drop attacks.
- Bloods: lamotrigine level, FBC, U&Es, LFTs, vitamin D, and ferritin to check for reversible contributors to fatigue.
- We discussed future options including structured psychological support for anxiety and stress-management strategies, and potential re-referral for video-EEG telemetry if functional impact remains high.
- Rescue plan reaffirmed for prolonged convulsive seizure or cluster, and guidance provided regarding when to seek urgent care.

I will review Thomas Reid in clinic in 3 months, or sooner if there is any escalation in convulsive frequency, injury, or concerns from the patient or carers.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned: 'one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month', 'daily absences', 'focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks', and 'drop attacks' (no explicit rate given for drops). Rule: choose the highest frequency across seizure types. 'Daily absences' implies the most frequent rate (at least 1 per day). No evidence of 6-month seizure freedom; recent note 'Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported' does not meet 6-month criterion and does not override daily absences. Therefore, final label is 1 per day, based on daily absences.
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per month, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal non-motor with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
